title: Event 1
content:
Summary Report: “Aws study group technical sharing” Event Objectives Introduce new knowledge regarding artificial intelligence (AI) and cloud computing. Highlight modern technology trends. Create opportunities for students to connect with industry experts. Gain practical insights and guidance on skill development aligned with business needs. Speakers Long Huynh Ly Kien Huy Thinh Nguyen Thao Nguyen phuong Highlights The event series features multiple sessions covering various topics, focusing on:
Prompt Engineering and optimization techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs). Strategies for learning from the successful models of modern technology platforms. Growth mindset and an attitude geared towards learning and personal development in the tech industry. An introduction to BMAD and AI tools that support software development, such as ChatGPT, Claude, GitHub Copilot, and Cursor AI. Key Takeaways Through the event, I gained a wealth of new knowledge, including:
Understanding the principles of crafting effective prompts for AI interaction. Learning various techniques to optimize the quality of AI model responses. Gaining insight into AI application trends within software development and modern workflows. Recognizing the importance of a learning mindset, adaptability, and continuous technological upskilling. Event Experience Participating in the “AWS Study Group Technical Sharing” workshop was a highly rewarding experience that gave me a comprehensive overview of application modernization using modern methods and tools. The event was lively, featuring engaging discussions and exchanges between the speakers and attendees. The content was presented in an accessible manner, blending theory with real-world examples; this made it easy for me to grasp new concepts and inspired me to delve deeper into AI and other technologies currently being adopted by businesses.
Practical Technical Experience Through the presentations, I gained exposure to various technologies and tools currently used in real-world applications, such as Large Language Models (LLMs), Prompt Engineering, AI agents, and AI-powered coding assistants. This knowledge has deepened my understanding of the process of integrating AI into software development and current digital transformation trends.
Kết nối và trao đổi Sự kiện tạo điều kiện để mình gặp gỡ và trao đổi với các diễn giả, sinh viên và những người có cùng mối quan tâm về AI và Cloud Computing. Qua đó, mình có thêm cơ hội mở rộng mối quan hệ, chia sẻ kinh nghiệm học tập và tìm hiểu về các định hướng nghề nghiệp trong lĩnh vực công nghệ.
Key Takeaways Through this series of events, I have realized that learning in the technology sector goes beyond classroom knowledge; it requires proactively staying updated on new trends, honing critical thinking skills, and leveraging AI tools to boost productivity. Furthermore, a mindset of continuous learning and the ability to adapt to technological changes are crucial factors for individual growth in the future.

title: Event 2
content:
Summary Report: “Event Cloud Architect” Event Objectives The Cloud Architect competition aims to provide an academic platform where students can reinforce their knowledge of cloud computing and cloud architecture design. Beyond testing technical expertise, the competition fosters teamwork, strategic thinking, and the ability to make decisions under time constraints.
Key Highlights The competition features head-to-head matchups between eight teams, each consisting of five members. Teams take turns answering questions ranging from basic to advanced levels, covering topics such as cloud computing and system architecture design. Additionally, teams can utilize special options like “Minimal Risk” and “Star of Hope,” adding a strategic and exciting dimension to the contest.

The atmosphere was lively and competitive, yet it also provided an opportunity for teams to network, learn from one another, and share experiences. Advancing through multiple rounds to secure Third Place was a meaningful milestone for the entire team.
Hands-on Technical Experience The competition enabled me to apply the knowledge I had acquired to real-world scenarios involving cloud computing and system architecture design. Through the process of discussing and solving problems with my teammates, I gained a deeper understanding of how to analyze requirements, select appropriate solutions, and develop a system design mindset.
Networking and Exchange Participating in the competition gave me the opportunity to interact with other teams and engage with the organizers on topics related to Cloud Computing. It was also a chance to network with like-minded individuals who share a passion for technology and similar academic interests.
Key Takeaways The competition made me realize that technical knowledge must be complemented by teamwork, logical thinking, and the ability to make quick decisions. Winning Third Prize was the result of effective collaboration among team members and diligent preparation. This achievement serves as motivation for me to continue learning, deepen my understanding of Cloud Computing, and actively participate in future academic activities.

title: Event 3
content: Event Recap – July 11 Event Objectives Explore and stay updated on the latest security solutions and tools within the AWS cloud ecosystem. Enhance understanding of Service Level Agreements (SLAs), risk management mechanisms, operational monitoring, and automated alerting systems. Clarify the exam structure, key knowledge areas, and optimal preparation strategies for the AWS Cloud Practitioner certification. Bridge the gap between academic theory and real-world infrastructure deployment scenarios. Define a learning and career development roadmap aligned with current technology trends. Speaker List Thinh Nguyen Nguyễn Huỳnh Sơn Ngô Lê Tuấn Huy Key Highlights Speaker 1: Optimizing Web Application Security via AWS Security Agent Identifying common security vulnerabilities and preventive solutions using “Edge Agents.” Source code review and security assessment processes integrated with automated penetration testing tools. Analysis of security investment costs and core technical limitations. Speaker 2: From Service Level Agreements (SLAs) to System Monitoring The nature of SLA metrics and the close relationship between monitoring activities and risk management strategies. Real-world case study: Understanding the fundamental differences between two system endpoints. Methods for converting metric data streams into timely incident alerts. Speaker 3: Roadmap to Achieving the AWS Cloud Practitioner Certification Overview of the exam format, assessment objectives, and the structure of the official exam sections. Deep dive into four foundational knowledge areas: Part 1: Cloud computing principles and models. Part 2: Information security, data protection, and compliance standards. Part 3: AWS service systems and technology solutions. Part 4: Pricing models, budget management, and technical support plans. Practical insights: Time allocation strategies, official study resources, and tips for achieving high scores on multiple-choice exams. Key Takeaways The session provided me with a deeper understanding of the importance of information security right from the software development stage. I also learned how to utilize automation tools to proactively scan for vulnerabilities and minimize system security risks. I gained practical insights into SLA operations and alerting systems. Monitoring metrics must align closely with core processes, prioritize the actual user experience, and ensure rapid response times during incidents. Additionally, I developed a comprehensive understanding of the AWS Cloud Practitioner certification. Achieving success requires a study plan that effectively integrates theoretical reinforcement, hands-on lab configuration, and skill practice using mock exams. Practical Application Strategy The knowledge gained will serve as a foundation for optimizing secure source code management, establishing strict access control policies, configuring monitoring systems, and developing automated response protocols for anomaly detection. A thorough understanding of these services enables the selection of appropriate solutions for specific challenges and effective resource cost management, thereby enhancing professional expertise and preparing for roles in the Cloud and DevOps fields. Event Experience This was an incredibly valuable, highly practical, and concise workshop. The technical content was conveyed in a visual and coherent manner, making it very accessible to both students and professionals just starting out in the field of AWS cloud computing. The event not only provided great motivation for me to continue honing my skills in Cloud/DevOps but also offered clear direction, giving me the confidence to map out my career path for the journey ahead. title: Blog 1
content:
Building Protein Research Copilot and Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Building a Protein Research Copilot with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Hello, AWS Study Group VN! While exploring AI Agents on AWS, I came across a fascinating use case: building a Protein Research Copilot using Amazon Bedrock AgentCore. This AI system assists protein researchers in searching for homologous peptides and analyzing biological data using nothing but natural language.
Previously, to identify proteins or peptides with similar structures, researchers had to:
Manually search through large datasets Run sequence comparison algorithms Analyze results themselves using biological expertise This process was time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to scale as data volumes grew.
With Protein Research Copilot, AWS combines AI Agents, Vector Search, and Foundation Models to automate the entire research workflow: Natural Language Query Parsing
“Find 10 peptides similar to dengue virus peptide LPAIVREAI” and convert it into a structured query.
Vector Similarity Search
Protein sequences are converted into embeddings using a specialized model (ESM-C 300M), followed by a search for similar peptides in a vector database.
AI-powered Summarization
After retrieving results, the AI ​​automatically summarizes them and provides scientific insights to the researcher. The system utilizes key AWS services:
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore — Runtime for deploying and scaling AI agents Amazon Bedrock — Runs LLMs for reasoning and summarization Amazon SageMaker — Deploys protein embedding models Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL + pgvector — Stores embeddings for semantic search The most notable point:
Instead of the AI ​​“memorizing” the entire protein dataset, the system employs Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to:
Retrieve the relevant data Provide context to the LLM Deliver more accurate answers and reduce hallucinations This helps shorten the research process from hours to just a few minutes.
Conclusion: Protein Research Copilot demonstrates that AI agents are not limited to chatbots but can also be applied to specialized fields such as biomedicine, pharmaceuticals, and scientific research. It serves as an excellent example of how AWS combines Agentic AI, vector databases, and LLMs to build practical AI systems at production scale. Nguồn tham khảo: https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/machine-learning/build-a-protein-research-copilot-with-amazon-bedrock-agentcore/

title: Blog 2
content:
An Introduction to Amazon Nova Forge — A Solution for Enterprises to Build Their Own Frontier Models Hello everyone in the AWS Study Group VN community. While self-studying AWS services, I came across an AWS blog post about Amazon Nova Forge. The first question that crossed my mind was: how can a business get an AI model that “understands” its internal data without incurring the massive costs of building one from scratch? After digging into the details, I realized that Nova Forge aims to solve exactly that problem, so I wanted to share my understanding of it.
Currently, the most common way for businesses to “teach” AI to understand internal data is through fine-tuning—essentially feeding company data into the model so it can learn the information. While this sounds promising, the issue is that the more new data is injected, the greater the risk that the model will “forget” the foundational skills it already possesses, such as reasoning, natural language understanding, and logical analysis. This phenomenon is known as “catastrophic forgetting.”
So, what about building a model from scratch? The costs run into the millions of dollars, requiring a team of experts and massive infrastructure—something not every business can manage. This is precisely the dilemma most enterprises face: the need for AI that deeply understands internal data, yet a lack of solutions that are both effective and cost-efficient. That is why Amazon Nova Forge was created.
Amazon Nova Forge is an AWS service that enables businesses to build their own frontier AI models, deeply trained on industry-specific data. Its key differentiator compared to traditional methods—such as superficial surface-level fine-tuning or costly development from scratch—is that it allows you to start from an existing Amazon Nova checkpoint (a model already pre-trained by AWS to a certain stage) and then continue training it using your own internal data.
Simply put: AWS has already handled the most difficult and costly aspects—you simply pick up where they left off and teach the model the specific details of your industry that it doesn’t yet know.
Nova Forge offers three different starting points, depending on the amount of internal data you have:
Pre-trained: The model possesses foundational knowledge—such as reading, writing, and reasoning—but lacks specialization in any specific field. This is suitable for companies with large volumes of internal data, as the model is at the stage where it is most ‘hungry’ for domain-specific knowledge. Mid-trained: The model is undergoing fine-tuning and is not yet fully finalized; it remains ‘open’ to absorbing further information. This is suitable for companies with a moderate amount of data. Post-trained: The model is fully developed and ready for deployment. This is suitable for companies that only wish to make minor adjustments to the model’s behavior without needing to retrain it on in-depth, specialized knowledge. A major risk when training AI on internal data is “catastrophic forgetting”—where newly ingested data overwrites the model’s existing knowledge, causing it to gradually lose foundational skills such as reasoning and natural language understanding. Nova Forge addresses this issue through data mixing: instead of training the model solely on the company’s internal data, AWS allows you to blend it with Amazon Nova’s standard data at an appropriate ratio.
Kết quả: model vừa học được kiến thức chuyên biệt của công ty, vừa giữ được nền tảng trí tuệ tổng quát mà nó đã có. Một ví dụ thực tế mà mình nghĩ đến ngay là thương mại điện tử. Hình dung bạn vào website của một shop mỹ phẩm, chatbot hỏi bạn về tình trạng da - da dầu, hay bị mụn - rồi tự động gợi ý đúng sản phẩm phù hợp với bạn. Chatbot đó không chỉ ’tìm kiếm’ mà thực sự hiểu dữ liệu sản phẩm, chính sách, và đặc thù khách hàng của shop đó - đó là thứ mà Nova Forge có thể giúp xây dựng.
Conclusion: Amazon Nova Forge enables businesses to build their own frontier models—trained extensively on data specific to their target industry—at a significantly lower cost than building them from scratch.
Nguồn:https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/aws/introducing-amazon-nova-forge-build-your-own-frontier-models-using-nova/

title: Create S3 Bucket
content:
Create S3 Bucket In this step, you will create 3 S3 buckets for the system:
Frontend Bucket: stores the build files for the React frontend. Audio Bucket: stores the audio/video files uploaded by users. Output Bucket: stores the transcript results generated by Amazon Transcribe. 1. Access Amazon S3 Sign in to the AWS Management Console. In the search bar, enter S3. Select the S3 service. 2. Create the Frontend Bucket Choose Create bucket. In Bucket name, enter: frontend In AWS Region, select the region you are using for the workshop, for example ap-southeast-1. In Object Ownership, keep the default value. In Block Public Access settings, keep all public access blocking options enabled. Keep the remaining settings as default. Choose Create bucket. 3. Create the Audio Bucket Choose Create bucket. Enter the bucket name: audio-bucket Select the same region as the Frontend Bucket. Keep Block all public access enabled. Choose Create bucket. 4. Create the Output Bucket Choose Create bucket. Enter the bucket name: output-bucket Select the same region as the other buckets. Keep Block all public access enabled. Choose Create bucket. 5. Configure CORS for the Audio Bucket The frontend will upload files directly to the Audio Bucket by using a presigned URL, so you need to configure CORS for this bucket.
Open the audio-bucket bucket. Select the Permissions tab. Scroll down to Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS). Choose Edit. Paste the following configuration: [ { "AllowedHeaders": ["*"], "AllowedMethods": ["PUT", "POST", "GET"], "AllowedOrigins": ["*"], "ExposeHeaders": ["ETag"] } ] Choose Save changes. 6. Configure Event Notification for the Audio Bucket After a user uploads a file, the Audio Bucket will send an event to an SQS Queue so that Lambda can process it asynchronously.
You can return to this step after creating the SQS Queue in section 5.2.3.
Open the audio-bucket bucket. Select the Properties tab. Scroll down to Event notifications. Choose Create event notification. Enter the event name: audio-uploaded-event In Event types, select All object create events. In Destination, select SQS queue. Select the audio-processing-queue queue. Choose Save changes. 7. Verify the Result After completing this step, you should have 3 buckets:
The Frontend Bucket used to deploy the React app. The Audio Bucket used to receive uploaded files. The Output Bucket used to store transcripts.

title: Introduction
content:
Introduction to the AWS Audio To Text project The AWS Audio To Text project is a serverless system on AWS that allows users to upload audio or video files such as MP3, MP4, WAV, and M4A to automatically convert speech content into text.
Users access the website through Amazon CloudFront, sign in with Amazon Cognito, and then upload files to Amazon S3 using a presigned URL. After upload, the audio file is processed automatically through S3 Event, SQS, Lambda, and Amazon Transcribe. The transcript result is stored in the S3 Output Bucket, the job status is stored in DynamoDB, and the system sends email notifications through Amazon SNS.
Workshop overview Web interface: React is built and stored in the Amazon S3 Frontend Bucket, then distributed through Amazon CloudFront so users can access the system over HTTPS. User management: Amazon Cognito handles registration, sign-in, and issues JWT tokens for user authentication when calling APIs. API gateway: Amazon API Gateway receives requests from the frontend, verifies Cognito tokens, and forwards the requests to the appropriate Lambda functions. File upload: lambda-upload-url generates a presigned URL so users can upload audio/video files directly to the Amazon S3 Audio Bucket, while also creating job information in DynamoDB. Input file storage: The Amazon S3 Audio Bucket stores MP3, MP4, WAV, or M4A files uploaded by users. Processing queue: Amazon SQS receives events from the S3 Audio Bucket after files are uploaded, allowing the system to process asynchronously and avoid overload. Conversion processing: lambda-processor reads messages from SQS and calls Amazon Transcribe to convert audio content into text. Result storage: The Amazon S3 Output Bucket stores the transcript result, including transcript.json generated by Transcribe and transcript.txt processed for easier reading by users. Result handling: lambda-result-handler reads the transcript.json file, creates transcript.txt, updates the job status in DynamoDB, and sends notifications via SNS. Status management: Amazon DynamoDB stores job information, processing status, user_id, file name, creation time, and transcript path. Result retrieval: lambda-get-job reads the job status from DynamoDB, retrieves transcript.txt from the S3 Output Bucket, and returns the result to the web interface. Conversion history: lambda-list-jobs retrieves the list of files uploaded and converted for the currently signed-in user. Notifications: Amazon SNS sends email notifications when the audio-to-text conversion process is completed. System monitoring: Amazon CloudWatch records logs from the Lambda functions to support inspection, debugging, and monitoring of system activity.

title: Proposal
content:
AWS Audio To Text Speech-to-Text Conversion System on AWS 1. Project Overview The AWS Audio To Text project is a serverless system built on AWS that allows users to upload audio or video files (such as MP3, MP4, WAV, or M4A) and automatically convert spoken content into text.
Users access the website via Amazon CloudFront, authenticate using Amazon Cognito, and upload files to Amazon S3 using presigned URLs. Once uploaded, files are automatically processed through an S3 event trigger, SQS, Lambda, and Amazon Transcribe. The resulting transcripts are stored in an S3 output bucket, job statuses are recorded in DynamoDB, and email notifications are sent via Amazon SNS.
In this workshop, the team will present the entire system-building process, covering architectural design, AWS service deployment, Lambda coding, React frontend integration, pipeline testing, and website deployment to CloudFront. AWS Audio To Text – An audio-to-text conversion system on AWS.
2. Objectives The primary objective of the project is to build an automated audio-to-text conversion system on the AWS platform.
Specific objectives include:
Build a serverless system: Utilize Lambda, S3, SQS, API Gateway, and DynamoDB to minimize server management. Support audio/video file uploads: Allow users to upload MP3, MP4, WAV, and M4A files. Convert audio to text: Use Amazon Transcribe to generate transcripts. Manage users: Use Amazon Cognito for user registration, login, and authentication. Store conversion history: Save metadata, job status, and transcript paths in DynamoDB. Send notifications: Use Amazon SNS to send email notifications upon job completion. Deploy the website online: Host the React frontend using an S3 bucket and CloudFront. Monitor and debug: Use CloudWatch Logs to track Lambda activity. 3. Problem to Solve Current Issue After online meetings, classes, or interviews, users often have to listen to entire audio or video files to take notes on key points. This process is time-consuming, prone to information loss, and difficult to manage when dealing with multiple files. Furthermore, audio/video files are often large; uploading them via a traditional backend can overload the system. Since converting audio to text is also time-consuming, there is a need for an automated, asynchronous, and user-secure processing system.
Solution The AWS Audio-to-Text system utilizes a serverless architecture on AWS to automatically convert audio into text. Users log in via Amazon Cognito and upload files directly to Amazon S3 using presigned URLs. Upon upload, an S3 Event sends a message to Amazon SQS, triggering AWS Lambda to call Amazon Transcribe for the audio-to-text conversion. The output is stored in an S3 Output Bucket, while processing status and history are saved in DynamoDB; additionally, Amazon SNS sends an email notification upon completion. The website is deployed using an S3 Frontend Bucket and CloudFront, enabling users to easily access the site, view transcripts, download results, and manage their file history.
4. Solution Architecture The system is designed based on a serverless model, comprising the following key layers: frontend, authentication, API, processing, storage, database, notification, and monitoring. AWS Services Used
Amazon CloudFront: Distributes the React website via HTTPS. Amazon S3: Stores React frontend build files, user-uploaded audio/video files, and transcript.json/transcript.txt files (across 3 buckets). Amazon Cognito: Manages registration, login, and JWT token issuance. Amazon API Gateway: Provides APIs for the frontend to communicate with the backend. AWS Lambda: Handles serverless backend logic (5 functions). Amazon SQS: Queues audio files for asynchronous processing. Amazon Transcribe: Converts audio to text. Amazon DynamoDB: Stores job details, processing status, and file history. Amazon SNS: Sends email notifications upon completion of processing. Amazon CloudWatch: Stores logs and supports Lambda debugging. Component Design Web Interface: The React application is built and hosted in an Amazon S3 frontend bucket, then distributed via Amazon CloudFront to allow user access to the system over HTTPS. User Management: Amazon Cognito handles registration and login, issuing JWT tokens to authenticate users during API calls. API Gateway: Amazon API Gateway receives requests from the frontend, validates Cognito tokens, and routes requests to the appropriate Lambda functions. File Upload: The lambda-upload-url function generates a presigned URL allowing users to upload audio/video files directly to the Amazon S3 audio bucket, while simultaneously creating a job record in DynamoDB. Input File Storage: The Amazon S3 audio bucket stores user-uploaded MP3, MP4, WAV, or M4A files. Processing Queue: Amazon SQS receives events from the S3 audio bucket following file uploads, enabling asynchronous processing and preventing system overload. Transcription processing: The lambda-processor reads messages from SQS and invokes Amazon Transcribe to convert audio content into text. Result storage: The Amazon S3 Output Bucket stores transcription results, including the transcript.json file generated by Transcribe and a transcript.txt file reformatted for readability. Result handling: The lambda-result-handler reads the transcript.json file, generates the transcript.txt file, updates the job status in DynamoDB, and sends a notification via SNS. State management: Amazon DynamoDB stores job details, processing status, user_id, file name, creation timestamp, and the transcription file path. Result retrieval: The lambda-get-job function retrieves the job status from DynamoDB and the transcript.txt content from the S3 Output Bucket, returning the result to the web interface. Transcription history: The lambda-list-jobs function retrieves the list of uploaded and transcribed files for the currently logged-in user. Notifications: Amazon SNS sends email notifications once the audio-to-text transcription process is complete. System monitoring: Amazon CloudWatch logs Lambda function activity to facilitate auditing, debugging, and system monitoring. 5. Implementation Schedule Day 1: Analyze requirements, define core functions, and create architecture and workflow diagrams Day 2: Create S3 buckets, Cognito User Pool, and App Client; configure user login Day 3: Set up DynamoDB, SQS, and API Gateway; configure IAM roles and policies for Lambda Day 4: Develop Lambda functions: lambda-upload-url, lambda-processor, and lambda-result-handler Day 5: Develop Lambda functions: lambda-get-job and lambda-list-jobs; integrate API Gateway with Cognito Authorizer Day 6: Develop React frontend; integrate Cognito; implement file upload, transcript display, and download button Day 7: Perform end-to-end testing; configure SNS email notifications; deploy frontend to S3 + CloudFront; verify CloudWatch Logs 6. Budget Estimate You can view the costs on the AWS Pricing Calculator
Infrastructure Costs
Amazon CloudFront: $0.12/month (1 GB data transfer out to the internet, 1,000 HTTPS requests/month). Amazon S3: $0.03/month (1 GB S3 Standard, 500 PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests, 1,000 GET/SELECT/other requests). Amazon Cognito: $0.00/month (10 monthly active users). Amazon API Gateway: $0.00/month (HTTP API: 0.001 million requests/month, equivalent to 1,000 requests; average size of 34 KB/request). AWS Lambda: $0.00/month (1,000 requests/month, x86 architecture, Buffered invoke mode, 512 MB ephemeral storage). Amazon SQS: $0.00/month (0.001 million Standard Queue requests/month, equivalent to 1,000 requests). Amazon Transcribe: $0.60/month (100 minutes of batch processing using standard Amazon Transcribe). Amazon DynamoDB: $0.03/month (Standard table class, 0.1 GB storage, 1 KB average item size). Amazon SNS: $0.00/month (1,000 requests/month, 1,000 EMAIL/EMAIL-JSON notifications). Amazon CloudWatch: $0.07/month (0.1 GB Standard Logs ingested). Total: $0.85/month, $10.20/12 months.
7. Risk Assessment Risk Matrix
IAM Error: High impact, medium probability. Lambda may be unable to access S3, DynamoDB, Transcribe, or SNS. CORS Error: Medium impact, high probability. The frontend may fail to call the API or upload files to S3. S3 Event/SQS Failure: High impact, medium probability. Files are uploaded successfully but fail to enter the processing pipeline. Transcribe error: High impact, medium probability. Incorrect file formats, poor audio quality, or incorrect language codes can prevent the system from generating transcripts. AWS budget overrun: Medium impact, low probability. Costs may increase if testing numerous large files or running multiple Transcribe jobs. Cognito error: Medium impact, medium probability. Users may be unable to log in, or the API may return authentication errors. Mitigation Strategies
IAM: Assign appropriate permissions to each Lambda function and check for errors via CloudWatch Logs. CORS: Configure CORS for API Gateway, the S3 Audio Bucket, and the S3 Output Bucket. S3 Event/SQS: Verify Event Notifications, SQS policies, and Lambda triggers. Transcribe: Restrict input to MP3, MP4, WAV, and M4A formats; test using short, clear audio files. Costs: Use small test files, set up AWS Budget Alerts, and delete unnecessary resources after the demo. Cognito: Verify the User Pool ID, App Client ID, JWT Authorizer, and Authorization header. Contingency Plan
If the automated pipeline fails, the team will manually check each step—from S3, SQS, Lambda, and Transcribe to DynamoDB. If the trigger fails to activate, the Lambda function can be tested using a sample event. If a frontend error occurs after deployment, the team will revert to running the application locally to verify the API first, then rebuild and redeploy to S3/CloudFront. If costs rise, the team will pause the trigger, delete test files, and review the billing details.

title: Blogs Posted
content:
Blog 1 - Building Protein Research Copilot and Amazon Bedrock AgentCore This is an AI system that assists protein researchers in searching for homologous peptides and analyzing biological data using only natural language.
Blog 2 - Exploring Amazon Nova Forge — A Solution For Building Custom Frontier Models For Enterprises This blog provides an overview of Amazon Nova Forge, a new AWS service that helps enterprises build their own AI models based on domain-specific data without having to retrain a model from scratch at a massive cost. You will learn why approaches such as fine-tuning or building a model from zero still have many limitations, especially the risk of Catastrophic Forgetting, where a model learns new data but loses its foundational capabilities. The article also explains how Nova Forge works through available Amazon Nova checkpoints, the Data Mixing mechanism that balances internal data with general knowledge, and a practical example of how enterprises can create an AI model that deeply understands their own industry, products, and customers.

title: Create SQS Queue
content:
Create SQS Queue In this step, you will create an SQS Standard Queue to receive file upload events from the S3 Audio Bucket.
SQS helps decouple the file upload process from the Transcribe processing workflow. When a file is uploaded to S3, the event is sent to the queue. Then, the Lambda Processor reads messages from the queue and starts converting the audio into text.
1. Access Amazon SQS Sign in to the AWS Management Console. In the search bar, enter SQS. Select Simple Queue Service. 2. Create a Queue In the SQS console, choose Create queue. In Type, select Standard. In Name, enter: audio-processing-queue 3. Configure the Queue Keep the following settings at their default values:
Visibility timeout Message retention period Delivery delay Maximum message size Receive message wait time 4. Configure the Access Policy for S3 In Access policy, select Advanced and paste the policy below.
Replace the following values before saving:
<account-id>: your AWS Account ID. <region>: the region you are using, for example ap-southeast-1. <input-bucket-name>: the name of the Audio Bucket created in section 5.2.1. { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "AllowS3SendMessageToAudioProcessingQueue", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "s3.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sqs:SendMessage", "Resource": "arn:aws:sqs:<region>:<account-id>:audio-processing-queue", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:SourceAccount": "<account-id>" }, "ArnLike": { "aws:SourceArn": "arn:aws:s3:::<input-bucket-name>" } } } ] } 5. Finish Creating the Queue Keep Redrive allow policy and Dead-letter queue at their default settings. Choose Create queue. After the queue is created, open the audio-processing-queue queue. Record the queue ARN to use when configuring the S3 Event Notification and Lambda trigger. 6. Verify the Connection with S3 After creating the queue, return to the Event Notification configuration for the Audio Bucket:
Open the S3 Audio Bucket. Go to the Properties tab. Create an event notification for All object create events. Select audio-processing-queue as the destination. Save the configuration. When a user uploads a file to the Audio Bucket, S3 will send a message to this queue for Lambda to process.

title: Deploy Frontend on CloudFront
content:
AWS Audio To Text Deploy Frontend on CloudFront In this section, you will build the React frontend, upload the build files to the S3 Frontend Bucket, and distribute the website with Amazon CloudFront.
After completing this section, users can access the system through the CloudFront URL, sign in with Cognito, upload audio/video files, and view transcript results.
1. Prepare Backend Information Before building the frontend, you need the following information from section 5.2:
API Gateway Invoke URL. Cognito User Pool ID. Cognito App Client ID. AWS Region. S3 Frontend Bucket name. Example:
VITE_HTTP_API_BASE=https://tr0pxa2af8.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod VITE_USER_ID=demo-user VITE_AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-1 VITE_COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_usOq0wncO VITE_COGNITO_APP_CLIENT_ID=es27s0fdh69m14ttvnkr7vrmi 2. Configure Frontend Environment Variables In the frontend source directory, create or update the .env file.
VITE_HTTP_API_BASE=https://tr0pxa2af8.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod VITE_USER_ID=demo-user VITE_AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-1 VITE_COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID=ap-southeast-1_usOq0wncO VITE_COGNITO_APP_CLIENT_ID=es27s0fdh69m14ttvnkr7vrmi If the frontend uses Create React App instead of Vite, change the VITE_ prefix to REACT_APP_ according to your project structure.
3. Install Dependencies Open a terminal in the frontend directory and run:
npm install 4. Run the Frontend Locally Run the frontend locally to test the Cognito and API Gateway connection.
npm run dev Or:
npm start Open a browser and access the local URL, for example:
http://localhost:5173 Check the following features:
Register or sign in with Cognito. Upload a short audio/video file. Check that a job is created in DynamoDB. Check that the file is uploaded to the S3 Audio Bucket. Check that a message goes to SQS and Lambda processes it. 5. Build the Frontend After the local test succeeds, build the frontend:
npm run build The build directory is usually:
dist if you use Vite. build if you use Create React App. 6. Upload Build Files to the S3 Frontend Bucket Because I have already done this before, I will guide you through the steps.
Access Amazon S3. Open the frontend-<account-id>-<region> bucket. Choose Upload. Upload all contents inside the build directory, not the parent directory itself. Choose Upload. Drag the assets folder and the index.html file, then choose Upload.
7. Create a CloudFront Distribution Access CloudFront. Choose Create distribution. In Choose a plan, select the Free plan. Choose Next. In Get started, set Distribution name to audio-to-text-frontend. Choose Next. In Specify origin, set Origin type to Amazon S3. For Origin, choose Browse S3, select the frontend bucket, and choose Next until you reach Review and create. In Review and create, do not change anything and choose Create distribution. After the distribution is created, you will receive a domain.
After opening the domain in a browser, you will see your website like this:
8. Update the Bucket Policy for CloudFront After creating the distribution, CloudFront will show the policy that needs to be added to the S3 bucket.
Copy the policy suggested by CloudFront. Open the S3 Frontend Bucket. Select the Permissions tab. In Bucket policy, choose Edit. Paste the policy and choose Save changes. { "Version": "2008-10-17", "Id": "PolicyForCloudFrontPrivateContent", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "AllowCloudFrontServicePrincipal", "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "s3:GetObject", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::frontend-594470794140-ap-southeast-1-an/*", "Condition": { "ArnLike": { "AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:cloudfront::594470794140:distribution/E21RVUI13QXFH2" } } } ] } 9. Update CORS After CloudFront finishes deploying, copy the Distribution domain name, for example:
d3cvbj64u8o2eh.cloudfront.net Update CORS:
API Gateway CORS: add the CloudFront domain to Access control allowed origin. 11. Test the AWS Audio To Text Project 1. Register a user.
2. Receive the verification code by email.
3. Enter the verification code.
4. Home page.
5. Select an audio file to convert to text.
6. Upload the file successfully.
7. Result after converting the audio file to text.

title: Clean Up Resources
content:
Clean Up Resources After completing the workshop, you should delete the resources you created to avoid unnecessary costs.
Resources to clean up include:
CloudFront Distribution S3 buckets and the objects inside them API Gateway Lambda functions SQS Queue SNS Topic and Subscription DynamoDB Table Cognito User Pool CloudWatch Log Groups IAM Role and IAM Policy created for Lambda Amazon Transcribe jobs that remain in the account 1. Delete the CloudFront Distribution Access CloudFront. Select the distribution used for the frontend. Choose Disable. Wait until the distribution status changes to Disabled. Choose Delete. CloudFront may take a few minutes to disable before it allows deletion.
2. Delete Objects in S3 Buckets Before deleting a bucket, you need to delete all objects inside it.
Do this for the 3 buckets:
frontend-<account-id>-<region> audio-bucket-<account-id>-<region> audio-output-<account-id>-<region> Steps:
Access Amazon S3. Open each bucket. Select all objects. Choose Delete. Enter permanently delete if the console asks for confirmation. 3. Delete S3 Buckets After the buckets are empty:
Return to the bucket list. Select each bucket. Choose Delete. Enter the bucket name to confirm. Choose Delete bucket. 4. Delete API Gateway Access API Gateway. Select the audio-to-text-api API. Choose Actions or the delete menu. Choose Delete. Confirm the API deletion. 5. Delete Lambda Functions Access Lambda and delete the following functions:
lambda-upload-url lambda-processor lambda-result-handler lambda-get-job lambda-list-jobs Steps:
Select the function you want to delete. Choose Actions. Choose Delete. Confirm the deletion. 6. Delete the SQS Queue Access Amazon SQS. Select the audio-processing-queue queue. Choose Delete. Confirm the queue deletion. 7. Delete the SNS Topic Access Amazon SNS. Select Topics. Select the job-completed-topic topic. Choose Delete. Confirm the topic deletion. 8. Delete the DynamoDB Table Access DynamoDB. Select Tables. Select the jobs table. Choose Delete. Confirm the table deletion. 9. Delete the Cognito User Pool Access Amazon Cognito. Select User pools. Open the workshop User Pool. Choose Delete user pool. Enter the User Pool name to confirm if the console asks. Choose Delete. 10. Delete CloudWatch Log Groups Each Lambda creates a log group in CloudWatch Logs. Delete these log groups to fully clean up the environment.
Access CloudWatch. Select Log groups. Find the following log groups: /aws/lambda/lambda-upload-url /aws/lambda/lambda-processor /aws/lambda/lambda-result-handler /aws/lambda/lambda-get-job /aws/lambda/lambda-list-jobs Select a log group. Choose Delete. 11. Delete IAM Role and Policy Access IAM. Select Roles. Find the role: audio-to-text-lambda-role Detach the policies attached to the role if needed. Choose Delete role. If you created an inline policy or a separate customer managed policy for the workshop, delete that policy after the role has been deleted.
12. Check Amazon Transcribe Access Amazon Transcribe. Select Transcription jobs. Delete test jobs if you do not need to keep them.

title: Create Cognito
content:
Create Cognito In this step, you will create an Amazon Cognito User Pool to manage user registration, sign-in, and authentication for the React frontend.
After a user signs in successfully, Cognito will issue a JWT token. The frontend uses this token to call API Gateway, and API Gateway uses a Cognito Authorizer to validate the request.
1. Access Amazon Cognito Sign in to the AWS Management Console. In the search bar, enter Cognito. Select Amazon Cognito. 2. Create a User Pool Because I have already created one, I will guide you through the steps.
Select User pools. Choose Create user pool. In Application type, select Single-page application (SPA). In Name your application, enter: User pool In Options for sign-in identifiers, select Email. In Self-registration, keep the default settings. In Required attributes for sign-up, choose email from Select attributes. Choose Create user directory. 3. Configure the App Client Because I have already created one, I will guide you through the steps.
Select App clients. Choose Create app client. In Application type, select Single-page application (SPA). In Name your application, set the name to audio-transcriber-user-pool. Choose Create app client. 6. Record the Cognito Information You need to record the following values to configure the frontend and API Gateway:
User Pool ID App Client ID

title: Create Lambda Functions and Configure Lambda
content:
Create Lambda Functions and Configure Lambda In this step, you will create the Lambda functions for the serverless backend of the AWS Audio To Text system.
The system uses 5 main Lambda functions:
lambda-upload-url: creates a presigned URL so the frontend can upload files to S3. lambda-processor: reads messages from SQS and calls Amazon Transcribe. lambda-result-handler: processes the transcript after Transcribe completes, updates DynamoDB, and sends SNS notifications. lambda-get-job: retrieves the details of a job and its transcript content. lambda-list-jobs: retrieves the job list for the currently signed-in user. 1. Access Lambda Sign in to the AWS Management Console. In the search bar, enter Lambda. Select Lambda. 2. Create the upload-url Lambda Because I have already done this before, I will guide you through the steps.
Open the Lambda service. Choose Create function. Select Author from scratch. Enter the function name: lambda-upload-url For Runtime, select Python 3.12. Choose Create function. 4. Configure Environment Variables for lambda-upload-url In the Configuration tab, select Environment variables. Choose Edit. Add the following configurations in Edit environment variables: AUDIO_BUCKET = <your-name-audio-bucket> JOBS_TABLE = jobs URL_EXPIRES_SECONDS = 900 Choose Save. This function receives file information from the frontend, creates a job_id, saves an item to DynamoDB, and returns a presigned URL.
5. Create the processor Lambda Create a new function with the following name:
lambda-processor Configure the role the same way as the previous step and add the following environment variables:
JOBS_TABLE = jobs LANGUAGE_CODE = vi-VN OUTPUT_BUCKET = <your-name-output-bucket> This function reads messages from SQS, retrieves file information from S3, calls Amazon Transcribe, and updates the job status to PROCESSING.
6. Attach an SQS Trigger to lambda-processor Open the lambda-processor function. In the Configuration tab, select Triggers. Choose Add trigger. In Source, select SQS. Select the audio-processing-queue queue. Keep Batch size as default or set it to 1 for easier debugging. Choose Add. After adding the trigger, the result will appear as shown in the image below.
7. Create the result-handler Lambda Create a new function with the following name:
lambda-result-handler Add the following environment variables:
CONNECTIONS_TABLE = ams-connections JOBS_TABLE = jobs OUTPUT_BUCKET = <your-name-output-bucket> SNS_TOPIC_ARN = <your-name-sns-topic> This function reads the transcript.json file, extracts the text content, creates a transcript.txt file, updates DynamoDB to COMPLETED, and sends an email notification through SNS.
8. Attach an S3 Trigger to lambda-result-handler Open the lambda-result-handler function. Choose Add trigger. Select S3 as the source. Select the Output Bucket. For Event type, select All object create events. Prefix: transcripts/, Suffix: .json. Choose Add. After adding the trigger, the result will appear as shown in the image below.
9. Create the get-job Lambda Create a new function with the following name:
lambda-get-job Add the following environment variables:
DOWNLOAD_URL_EXPIRES_SECONDS = 900 JOBS_TABLE = jobs OUTPUT_BUCKET = <your-name-output-bucket> This function receives a job_id, checks the job in DynamoDB, and returns the status along with the transcript content if it has completed.
10. Create the list-jobs Lambda Create a new function with the following name:
lambda-list-jobs Add the following environment variables:
JOBS_TABLE = jobs This function retrieves the list of jobs that belong to the currently signed-in user so the conversion history can be displayed on the frontend.
11. Check CloudWatch Logs After creating the functions, open the Monitor tab of each Lambda and choose View CloudWatch logs.
When testing the pipeline, CloudWatch Logs help you check:
Whether Lambda receives the correct event. Whether Lambda can read from S3, SQS, and DynamoDB. Whether the Transcribe job is created successfully. Whether SNS sends the notification successfully.

title: Workshop
content:
AWS Audio To Text Overview The AWS Audio To Text project is a serverless system on AWS that allows users to upload audio or video files such as MP3, MP4, WAV, and M4A, then automatically convert the speech content into text.
Users access the website through Amazon CloudFront, sign in with Amazon Cognito, and then upload files to Amazon S3 by using a presigned URL. After an audio file is uploaded, it is processed automatically through an S3 Event, SQS, Lambda, and Amazon Transcribe. The transcript result is stored in an S3 Output Bucket, the job status is stored in DynamoDB, and the system sends email notifications through Amazon SNS.
In this workshop, the team will present the complete process of building the system, from designing the architecture and deploying AWS services to writing Lambda code, integrating the React frontend, testing the pipeline, and deploying the website to CloudFront.
AWS Services Used in the Project Amazon CloudFront: Distributes the React website over HTTPS. Amazon S3: Stores the React frontend build files, uploaded user audio/video files, and transcript.json and transcript.txt files across 3 buckets. Amazon Cognito: Manages user registration, sign-in, and JWT token issuance. Amazon API Gateway: Provides APIs for the frontend to call the backend. AWS Lambda: Handles serverless backend logic with 5 functions. Amazon SQS: Provides an asynchronous queue for processing audio files. Amazon Transcribe: Converts audio into text. Amazon DynamoDB: Stores job information, processing status, and file history. Amazon SNS: Sends email notifications when processing is complete. Amazon CloudWatch: Stores logs and supports Lambda debugging. Content Introduction Prerequisites Deploy Frontend on CloudFront Clean Up Resources

title: Create SNS
content:
Create SNS In this step, you will create an Amazon SNS Topic to send email notifications when the audio-to-text conversion process is complete.
The lambda-result-handler Lambda will publish a message to the SNS Topic after the transcript is created successfully.
1. Access Amazon SNS Sign in to the AWS Management Console. In the search bar, enter SNS. Select Simple Notification Service. 2. Create a Topic Because I have already done this before, I will guide you through the steps.
In the left menu, select Topics. Choose Create topic. In Type, select Standard. In Name, enter: job-completed-topic Keep the remaining settings as default. Choose Create topic. 3. Create an Email Subscription Open the job-completed-topic topic. Choose Create subscription. In Protocol, select Email. In Endpoint, enter the email address that will receive notifications. Choose Create subscription. 4. Confirm the Email Open the email inbox you entered. Find the confirmation email from AWS Notifications. Choose Confirm subscription. Return to SNS and verify that the subscription status has changed to Confirmed. 5. Record the Topic ARN Open the topic and record the ARN to configure the lambda-result-handler Lambda.
SNS_TOPIC_ARN=arn:aws:sns:<region>:<account-id>:audio-transcription-notification 6. Send a Test Notification In the topic, choose Publish message. Enter the subject: AWS Audio To Text Test Enter the message: SNS notification test for AWS Audio To Text workshop. Choose Publish message. Check that the test notification was received in your email.

title: Create API Gateway
content:
Create API Gateway In this step, you will create Amazon API Gateway so the React frontend can call the backend Lambda functions.
API Gateway will provide the main endpoints:
POST /upload-url: creates a presigned URL to upload files. GET /jobs: retrieves the user’s job list. GET /jobs/{job_id}: retrieves job details and the transcript. The APIs will be protected by a Cognito JWT Authorizer.
1. Access API Gateway Sign in to the AWS Management Console. In the search bar, enter API Gateway. Select API Gateway. 2. Create an HTTP API Because I have already done this before, I will guide you through the steps.
Select the APIs tab. Choose Create API. In HTTP API, choose Build. In API name, enter: meeting-transcriber-http-api Temporarily skip adding an integration if the console asks for one. Choose Next until you reach the review step. Choose Create. 3. Create Lambda Integrations In the API you just created:
Select Integrations. Choose Manage integrations. Choose Create. For Integration type, select Lambda function. Select the region you are using. Select the corresponding Lambda function. Repeat these steps to create integrations for the following Lambda functions: lambda-upload-url lambda-get-job lambda-list-jobs 4. Create the POST /upload-url Route Select Routes. Choose Create. For Method, select POST. For Path, enter: /upload-url Select the lambda-upload-url integration. Choose Create. 5. Create the GET /jobs Route Choose Create route. For Method, select GET. For Path, enter: /jobs Select the lambda-list-jobs integration. Choose Create. 6. Create the GET /job Route Choose Create route. For Method, select GET. For Path, enter: /job Select the lambda-get-job integration. Choose Create. 7. Configure the Cognito JWT Authorizer In API Gateway, select Authorization. Choose Manage authorizers. Choose Create. For Authorizer type, select JWT. Name: cognito-jwt-authorizer Identity source: $request.header.Authorization Issuer URL: https://cognito-idp.<region>.amazonaws.com/<user-pool-id> For Audience, enter the Cognito App Client ID. Choose Create. After it is created, the result will appear as shown in the image below.
8. Attach the Authorizer to Routes Select each route: POST /upload-url GET /jobs GET /job In Authorization, choose Detach authorizer. Select cognito-jwt-authorizer, then choose Attach authorizer. Choose Save. 9. Configure CORS Select CORS. Choose Configure. Add the Allowed origins: http://localhost:5173 http://127.0.0.1:5173 After deploying the frontend to CloudFront, you will add the CloudFront domain to this list.
Allowed methods: GET,POST,OPTIONS Allowed headers: Authorization,Content-Type Choose Save. 10. Record the Invoke URL Select Stages. Open the default stage, usually $default. Record the Invoke URL.
